macro_events.event_summary: Oil executives warn that an Iran war is reshaping global energy markets, implying sustained higher oil prices, supply risk premia, and a structural shift toward energy security.
primary_drivers: Crude supply disruption risk premium (Strait of Hormuz / Iranian barrels offline)
Increased demand for non-Middle East energy supply (US shale, LNG, refining capacity)
Higher defense and shipping/insurance costs from regional conflict

beneficiaries.rationale: Largest US integrated with significant upstream leverage to Brent; ~40% of earnings from upstream means direct EPS uplift on sustained higher crude. Limited Iran/Hormuz operational exposure relative to European majors.
Permian-weighted upstream production benefits directly from higher realized crude prices; minimal Iranian operational exposure. Historically high beta to Brent shocks.
Pure-play Permian producer with ~100% US onshore production; every $10 sustained Brent move drops nearly fully to FCF given low hedge ratios in 2026.
Cheniere is the largest US LNG exporter; Middle East conflict raises European/Asian premium for non-Gulf LNG cargoes and supports long-term contract pricing power.
US onshore producer with high oil-weighted production mix; direct beneficiary of sustained crude premium with no Iran exposure.
Prime contractor for THAAD, PAC-3 Patriot interceptors and missiles being consumed/replenished in Middle East theater; restocking orders likely accelerate FY26 backlog.
Raytheon supplies Patriot, SM-3, AMRAAM — all heavily used in regional missile defense; replenishment contracts and allied FMS demand rise structurally.
Product tanker rates spike when Hormuz transit risk forces ton-mile re-routing around Africa; Scorpio is largest pure-play product tanker with high spot exposure.

losers.rationale: Jet fuel is ~25% of operating cost; Delta has historically lower hedging than peers, so sustained Brent above $90 directly compresses margins. Also has trans-Atlantic routes potentially affected by airspace closures.
International long-haul exposure (including Tel Aviv, Gulf routings) plus unhedged jet fuel cost — double hit from rerouting and fuel price.
Southwest is unhedged on fuel as of 2026 and runs thin domestic margins; every $10 in crude is a material EPS hit with limited fare pass-through in leisure-heavy network.
Carnival's bunker fuel costs rise with crude; Mediterranean/Middle East itineraries (a meaningful share of summer 2026 capacity) face cancellations and rerouting costs.
Naphtha/ethane feedstock costs rise with crude complex; commodity chemicals have limited ability to pass through in weak global demand backdrop.
Diesel/freight cost inflation hits trucking and last-mile costs; consumer discretionary spending pressured by higher gasoline prices at the pump squeezes low-income core customer.
Halliburton has meaningful Middle East services exposure (Saudi, Iraq, UAE) — escalation risks operational disruption and contract delays even as US activity may rise, creating mixed near-term setup.

headlines: The Iran war will change global energy markets in these important ways, oil executives say

macro_events.event_summary: Wall Street is promoting a "NACHO" trade thesis betting on higher oil prices and persistent/sticky inflation.
primary_drivers: Higher crude oil prices lift E&P revenue and reserves valuations
Persistent inflation supports real assets, commodities, and pricing-power names while pressuring long-duration assets
Higher-for-longer rate expectations compress growth multiples and lift bank NIMs

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with high leverage to crude price; ~60% of earnings tied to upstream oil & gas realizations.
Direct upstream oil price exposure via Permian and offshore production; cash flow scales linearly with WTI/Brent.
Highest-beta large-cap E&P to oil; Permian pure-play with elevated operating leverage to crude above $80.
Copper/gold producer benefits from persistent inflation thesis as real-asset hedge; commodity basket trade.
Largest gold miner; gold typically bid in sticky-inflation regimes as monetary debasement hedge.
Oilfield services leader; higher sustained crude drives upstream capex and rig activity globally.
Low-cost shale producer with pricing leverage; FCF grows materially with each $5/bbl move in WTI.

losers.rationale: Airline with jet fuel as ~25-30% of opex and limited hedging; higher crude compresses margins directly.
Major airline highly sensitive to jet fuel costs; every $10/bbl increase materially reduces EPS.
Low-margin retailer with consumer staples mix; sustained inflation pressures real wages and discretionary basket size, while fuel raises logistics cost.
Cruise operator with heavy bunker fuel exposure and discretionary consumer demand; double-hit from oil and inflation-pressured consumer.

headlines: Introducing the ‘NACHO’ trade: How Wall Street is betting on higher oil prices and persistent inflation
